full_transcript.speaker: Mark Jeffrey
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I mean, it's really the decentralized Coinbase, right? Yes, everything you said is correct, but a lot of people don't know what an automated market maker is. It is technically called that, an AMM. But the thing that was really that was happening before Uniswap, if you wanted to get your coin listed on a—you had to first of all get it on a centralized exchange. There was no other option, which meant you had to pay Binance or Coinbase or Kraken or somebody usually like a million bucks to get the listing, right? And then you had to provide your own market making, which meant that you had to provide a bunch of other coins to trade against your coin when it listed on the exchange. The exchange would not provide that liquidity, right? So along came Uniswap and they said, why don't we do something like a Coinbase or a Kraken or a Binance, but the entire thing is just powered by smart contracts and lives on the chain? Nobody owns it, nobody controls it except for the smart contract, and we attract liquidity by promising some portion of the trading fees to anyone who provides the liquidity to the smart contracts. So anyone could become a fractional owner through providing liquidity of an exchange, of this decentralized exchange. So you had two real big advantages: one, you had permissionless listing. Anybody could list any coin so long as they were able to define the swap pairs on Uniswap. And two, the community provided the liquidity. And this worked extraordinarily well and is basically the backbone of all DeFi today.

If you're trying to get 20 minutes or you're just trying to make a quick buck of something that's already in production, the economics change really dramatically. And we've validated this with seven or eight different data center clients who all say basically the key thing is they want to be able to take the compute off you, and there's no training method in the world that allows you to have the level of interruptibility we're designing for. Because typically training, you know, in a data center is something called synchronous training. So all of the nodes move in lockstep and it's actually the slowest node that sort of drives the speed of the whole system. We're trying to design for this world where we assume that Mark will slam his laptop shut, we assume that CoreWeave will take their rack back and sell it to a premium customer. We actually assume that in, sort of, even the case of large labs, you might just have an inference spike and you want to reallocate training compute to revenue workloads and then pull it back when you come down. Like, you know, my father was an energy engineer and you always design for the sort of peak load in the sinusoidal, and we're trying to make training compute liquid so that we can sort of achieve ultimate economic value really.

full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, maybe. But she has an incredible performance where she goes and talks with extreme passion about Staples and photocopies and pens and journals. And she literally clanks her nails and snaps up her favorite office products. And she's probably done more for the brand of Staples with young people and all the way up to Gen X myself, than Staples could ever do with a hundred million dollar ad campaign. I don't know what they're paying her.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Are they paying her?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I don't... is Staples embracing her?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: She's a store employee, Lon.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Oh, but yeah, but they're not paying her to make these videos, she's... they're just paying her to work the register.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I think now they are paying her. I think they must be paying her now. If not, there's a hundred people who would hire her to be the Walmart baddie or the Target baddie. I mean, she should be getting paid a million dollars a year. Minimum. Minimum of a million dollars a year. If one of my startups came to me and said "We want to hire this person to run our social media for a hundred thousand dollars a year plus nine hundred thousand dollars a year in stock options", I would say "Do it, do it immediately". Because the ability to authentically engage and then get me to buy the pen and know who the Staples Baddie is, is just infinitely valuable.

full_transcript.speaker: Mark Jeffrey
full_transcript.text: Yeah, really.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think I found her TikTok. She has 582.3 thousand followers on TikTok. That's crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I mean, somebody's gotta post her.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: Just for talking about Staples.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: There's a... there's a Papa John's guy too, right? I've seen him a bunch of times on TikTok, where he's just a guy who works at Papa John's, but he talks about how they make great pizza, which I don't...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I think now this is gonna become a thing. All right. Another amazing episode of TWIST is in the can for Monday, April 13th. Thanks, Mark Jeffrey.

highlights: BitTensor is described as the "Linux approach" to artificial intelligence, offering a decentralized alternative to centralized giants by incentivizing a global ecosystem of specialized subnets to compete on high-value applications.

The $10 million "rug pull" by a prominent subnet owner highlights a critical vulnerability in the ecosystem, prompting new proposals for smart contract-based token lock-ups to ensure founder accountability and capital conviction.

The IOTA subnet aims to make frontier-scale AI training more cost-efficient by orchestrating idle, "fractal" compute from consumer devices and data centers, effectively democratizing the massive GPU power typically reserved for the world's largest companies.

The BitMind subnet utilizes a "steel-on-steel" competition where one group of miners is incentivized to detect deepfakes while another is paid to generate them, creating a self-improving security loop that stays ahead of new generative models.

Decentralized AI protocols provide a unique opportunity for unaccredited investors to gain liquid exposure to early-stage technology startups, bypassing traditional venture capital barriers while maintaining the ability to exit at any time.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* centers on a major controversy within the Bittensor (TAO) ecosystem involving a "rug pull" by Sam Dare, the creator of the Templar subnet. Dare allegedly liquidated roughly $10 million in TAO tokens and abandoned his projects, claiming his exit was an act of "activism" against the perceived centralization of the network. Guest Mark Jeffrey and the hosts dismiss these claims as a rationalization for an exit scam, emphasizing that the incident highlights a need for more robust smart contract safeguards. To prevent future occurrences, they discuss technical solutions like "owner lock-ups," where subnet owners would be unable to liquidate their tokens while actively managing a project.

Despite the drama, the conversation highlights the innovative "AI Linux" potential of Bittensor through interviews with successful subnet founders. Ken Miyachi of BitMind (Subnet 34) explains how his project uses a competitive, adversarial mining model to detect deepfakes and provide "proof of human" services. Meanwhile, Will Squires and Stefan Cruz of Macrocosmos discuss their IOTA subnet (Subnet 9), which aims to democratize the training of frontier-scale AI models by utilizing idle consumer compute. This "train-at-home" concept allows everyday users to contribute their hardware to a global supercluster, competing with the massive, expensive data centers currently dominated by Big Tech.

The overarching theme of the transcript is the power of incentive-aligned decentralized networks. The participants argue that while the system is currently "ferocious capitalism" prone to growing pains, it offers a unique opportunity for non-accredited investors and individuals to participate in the economic upside of the AI revolution. The episode concludes with an "off-duty" segment exploring the intersection of authenticity and spectacle in modern performance, comparing Justin Bieber’s YouTube-themed Coachella set to the viral marketing success of the "Staples Baddie" on TikTok.
